content.text: Solve 5 of the following problems; at least one from each section.

body.text: Let \(X\) be an uncountable set and let \(F:X\to X\) be a function. Show that there are distinct elements \(x,y\in X\) such that \(x\ne F(y)\) and \(y\ne F(x)\).
Let \(P\) be a forcing. Show that there is an uncountable cardinal \(\kappa\) such that \(P\Vdash \check{\kappa}\) is a cardinal.
Provide an example of a subset of a Polish space which is analytic and not Borel. Provide a proof of these two properties of your set.
Give an example of a subset of \(\mathbb N\) that is \(\Pi^0_1\) and not \(\Sigma^0_1\) (and prove that your set has these two properties).
Prove that there exist incomparable Turing degrees.
Prove that there exist disjoint computably enumerable sets \(A,B\subseteq\mathbb N\) such that there is no computable set \(C\) which contains \(A\) and is disjoint from \(B\).

Let \(\mathcal L\) be a language comprised of a single binary relation symbol. Give an example of a countably infinite \(\mathcal L\)-structure \(\mathcal M\) such that. Make sure to prove both statements (i) and (ii).

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: \(\operatorname{Th}(\mathcal M)\) does not have quantifier elimination.

body.text: Let \(\mathcal L\) be a language comprised of two unary relation symbols \(G\) and \(B\), and a binary relation symbol \(R\). Let \(\mathcal K\) be the class of all finite graphs with a bipartition, i.e., \(\mathcal K\) consists of all finite \(\mathcal L\)-structures \(\mathcal M=(M,G^{\mathcal M},B^{\mathcal M},R^{\mathcal M})\) such that. Prove or disprove: \(\mathcal K\) is a Fraïssé class.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: \(R^{\mathcal M}\) is a symmetric and irreflexive relation on \(M\),

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: \(G^{\mathcal M}\) and \(B^{\mathcal M}\) form a partition of \(M\),

subparts.label: (iii)
subparts.text: if \((x,y)\in R^{\mathcal M}\) then exactly one of \(x,y\) belongs to \(G^{\mathcal M}\) and the other belongs to \(B^{\mathcal M}\).

body.text: Let \(\mathcal L=\{R\}\) be the language of graphs (i.e., \(R\) is a binary relation symbol). Let \(\mathcal K_0\) be the class of all connected graphs, and let \(\mathcal K_1\) be the class of all disconnected graphs. Prove or disprove each of the following:
